meta.name: PCL-5 PTSD Checklist
meta.description: The PCL-5 is a 20-item self-report measure assessing DSM-5 PTSD symptom severity across four clusters: intrusion, avoidance, negative alterations in cognition and mood, and alterations in arousal and reactivity.
meta.author: Weathers et al. (2013)
meta.reference: Weathers FW, Litz BT, Keane TM, Palmieri PA, Marx BP, Schnurr PP. The PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5). National Center for PTSD; 2013.
meta.instruction: Below is a list of problems that people sometimes have in response to a very stressful experience. Please read each problem carefully and then indicate how much you have been bothered by that problem in the past month.

questions.text: Repeated, disturbing, and unwanted memories of the stressful experience
Repeated, disturbing dreams of the stressful experience
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again (as if you were actually back there reliving it)
Feeling very upset when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience (for example, heart pounding, trouble breathing, sweating)
Avoiding memories, thoughts, or feelings related to the stressful experience
Avoiding external reminders of the stressful experience (for example, people, places, conversations, activities, objects, or situations)
Trouble remembering important parts of the stressful experience
Having strong negative beliefs about yourself, other people, or the world
Blaming yourself or someone else for the stressful experience or what happened after it
Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame
Loss of interest in activities that you used to enjoy
Feeling distant or cut off from other people
Trouble experiencing positive feelings (for example, being unable to feel happiness or have loving feelings for people close to you)
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Being superalert or watchful or on guard
Feeling jumpy or easily startled
Having difficulty concentrating
Trouble falling or staying asleep

ranges.label: Below PTSD Threshold
ranges.description: Your PTSD symptom score is below the clinical diagnostic threshold. While you may have experienced a traumatic event, your current symptom level does not suggest a diagnosis of PTSD. Some post-trauma reactions are normal and may resolve on their own.
suggestions: Continue using healthy coping strategies to process your experiences
Maintain regular routines, social connections, and physical activity
Monitor your symptoms, as they can sometimes emerge or worsen weeks or months after a traumatic event
If specific symptoms begin to cause distress, consider talking to a professional

ranges.label: Subthreshold PTSD Symptoms
ranges.description: Your score is near the clinical threshold for PTSD (33 points). You may be experiencing some PTSD symptoms such as intrusive memories, avoidance of reminders, negative mood changes, or heightened arousal that are causing some distress but may not meet the full diagnostic criteria.
suggestions: Consider seeking evaluation from a professional experienced in trauma
Learn about common trauma reactions to understand your experiences
Practice grounding techniques for managing intrusive memories or flashbacks
Maintain social connections and avoid isolating yourself
Monitor whether symptoms are stable, improving, or worsening over time

ranges.label: Probable PTSD
ranges.description: Your score is above the clinical cutoff of 33, suggesting probable PTSD. At this level, you are likely experiencing significant symptoms across intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, and arousal domains that are interfering with your daily functioning and quality of life.
suggestions: Seek professional evaluation from a trauma-specialized therapist
Evidence-based treatments for PTSD include Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), Prolonged Exposure (PE), and EMDR
Review your subscale scores below to understand which symptom clusters are most prominent
If you are experiencing nightmares or sleep disturbance, discuss these specifically with your provider
PTSD is very treatable; most people improve significantly with appropriate therapy

ranges.label: Severe PTSD Symptoms
ranges.description: Your score indicates severe PTSD symptoms that are likely causing profound impairment in your daily functioning, relationships, and overall well-being. At this level, comprehensive professional treatment is essential.
suggestions: Seek professional help from a trauma-specialized therapist as soon as possible
If you are in crisis or having thoughts of self-harm, contact a crisis helpline immediately (e.g., 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Evidence-based trauma therapies (CPT, PE, EMDR) are effective even for severe PTSD
Inform a trusted person about what you are experiencing; social support is important for recovery
Focus on safety and basic self-care while engaging in professional treatment

intrusion.label: Minimal
intrusion.description: Minimal intrusion symptoms. You are not significantly troubled by unwanted memories, nightmares, or flashbacks related to the traumatic event.

intrusion.label: Mild
intrusion.description: Mild intrusion symptoms. You may occasionally experience unwanted memories, distressing dreams, or brief moments of re-experiencing the traumatic event.

intrusion.label: Moderate
intrusion.description: Moderate intrusion symptoms. Intrusive memories, nightmares, or flashbacks may be occurring frequently and causing significant distress. These symptoms reflect the mind's difficulty processing the traumatic experience.

intrusion.label: Severe
intrusion.description: Severe intrusion symptoms. You are likely frequently troubled by vivid, unwanted re-experiencing of the trauma through memories, nightmares, or flashbacks. This level of intrusion typically requires professional trauma-focused therapy.

avoidance.label: Minimal
avoidance.description: Minimal avoidance. You are not significantly avoiding thoughts, feelings, or external reminders associated with the traumatic event.

avoidance.label: Mild
avoidance.description: Mild avoidance. You may be making some effort to avoid thoughts, memories, or situations that remind you of the trauma, which may slightly limit your activities.

avoidance.label: Moderate
avoidance.description: Moderate avoidance. You are actively avoiding trauma-related thoughts, feelings, and situations. This avoidance may be restricting your daily activities and social engagement.

avoidance.label: Severe
avoidance.description: Severe avoidance. You are extensively avoiding anything associated with the trauma, which is likely significantly restricting your life. While avoidance provides short-term relief, it maintains PTSD symptoms long-term.

cognition_mood.label: Minimal
cognition_mood.description: Minimal negative changes in thoughts and mood related to trauma. Your thinking patterns and emotional state do not appear significantly affected by the traumatic experience.

cognition_mood.label: Mild
cognition_mood.description: Mild negative changes in cognitions and mood. You may experience some negative beliefs about yourself or the world, reduced interest in activities, or emotional numbness since the traumatic event.

cognition_mood.label: Moderate
cognition_mood.description: Moderate negative cognitive and mood changes. You may have persistent negative beliefs, feelings of detachment, difficulty experiencing positive emotions, or distorted thoughts about the cause of the trauma.

cognition_mood.label: Severe
cognition_mood.description: Severe negative cognitive and mood changes. You are likely experiencing pervasive negative beliefs, emotional numbness, estrangement from others, and persistent inability to experience positive emotions. These changes significantly affect your quality of life.

arousal.label: Minimal
arousal.description: Minimal arousal and reactivity changes. You do not appear significantly affected by hypervigilance, startle responses, or other arousal-related PTSD symptoms.

arousal.label: Mild
arousal.description: Mild arousal symptoms. You may notice some increased irritability, difficulty concentrating, sleep problems, or heightened awareness of your surroundings since the traumatic event.

arousal.label: Moderate
arousal.description: Moderate arousal symptoms. You are likely experiencing notable hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response, sleep disturbance, or irritability that interferes with your daily functioning and relationships.

arousal.label: Severe
arousal.description: Severe arousal symptoms. You are likely in a persistent state of heightened alertness with significant sleep disruption, irritability, concentration problems, and exaggerated startle responses. This level of arousal is exhausting and requires professional support.
